item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
I have translated it some month ago into italian.
Promoved.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Flickr
License: Public Domain
Title: Joensuu broadcasting station, equipment made in Yleisradio's workshop in Helsinki, 1938
Author: Archives of the Finnish Broadcasting Company Yle
Release year: 1938

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted just because you never state the temperature keeps going down after it reaches -10 until the addenda, and that's a pretty major oversight that detracts from an otherwise good article. It's easily fixable though.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Turns out, this chill mammoth has actually been a pretty popular article to link to, both as part of the Wikiwalk, and by original inclusion by several authors. Linking back to a few of these articles would therefore make sense. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Once SCP-2082's effect has covered an area greater than 20 square meters, the area around it will exude large amounts of ice and sleet from all solid surfaces. In addition, SCP-2082's area of effect will increase to a radius of 1km 4 minutes after this threshold is reached. The sleet and ice will be continuously produced by SCP-2082 until the affected area resembles blizzard conditions. SCP-2082 is immune to the direct results of its own effect.

To this:

Once SCP-2082's effect has covered an area greater than 20 square meters, the area around it will exude large amounts of ice and sleet from all solid surfaces, in a manner similar to SCP-649. In addition, SCP-2082's area of effect will increase to a radius of 1km 4 minutes after this threshold is reached. The sleet and ice will be continuously produced by SCP-2082 until the affected area resembles blizzard conditions. SCP-2082 is immune to the direct results of its own effect.

Changing this:

Development of the technology which resulted in the creation of SCP-2082 began in 1962, when the Foundation developed cloning technology to recreate specimens of SCP-2082, by use of the tissue and other frozen preserved remains which had been purchased from museums and [REDACTED]. The project was led and directed by Dr. Ascher, and a small team of researchers at Research Area 908. Between October, 1962 and June, 1983, they attempted to create a replica of SCP-2082 using technological means. The technology used to create SCP-2082 was not anomalous, and the purpose of the research project was intended to shed light on the process of cloning.

To this:

Development of the technology which resulted in the creation of SCP-2082 began in 1962, when the Foundation developed cloning technology to recreate specimens of SCP-2082, by use of the tissue and other frozen preserved remains which had been purchased from museums and acquired from SCP-646, SCP-2683, and SCP-2706. The project was led and directed by Dr. Ascher, and a small team of researchers at Research Area 908. Between October, 1962 and June, 1983, they attempted to create a replica of SCP-2082 using technological means. The technology used to create SCP-2082 was not anomalous, and the purpose of the research project was intended to shed light on the process of cloning.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
two years later…

<Roget|Hockey> you're welcome to edit any of my SCPs, anytime, if you come across something in reading one or summat
<Voct> sure
<Voct> however
<Voct> "What killed the dinosaurs?"
<Voct> is REALLY a bad name for that one.
<Roget|Hockey> I know it's a stupid joke
<Voct> yeah, but there's not enough context for it to be clear that you know it's a joke
<Voct> how about
<Voct> Elephas cryophilas?
<Voct> cryophilus, rather
<Roget|Hockey> ok
<Roget|Hockey> done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't lie here, I upvoted purely for the title. 10/10 reference fantastic job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, at least I can assure this SCP didn't kill dinosaur.

Dinosaur's collapse was 65.50 MegaYrs ago.
The beginning of ice age was 1.1 MegaYrs ago.
Wooly mammoth was divided from Step mammoth about 1.5 MegaYrs ago.

A subspecies of Wooly mammoth cannot be the dinosaur killer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somehow, I was just expecting someone to say this.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST
item.content:encoded:
I'm honestly a bit appalled at the level of venom displayed in this discussion. A simple PM politely telling me I'm an idiot, with some proper argumentation thrown in for measure would have served far better than this bickering.

Anyway, yes, this conversation between me and Roget took place. Yes, after hearing him out, I gave him the go ahead to remove unclassed from neutralized articles, being under the impression that every neutralized article had once been classed S/E/K and should therefore not be tagged unclassed, but S/E/K..

The italicized part is where I fucked up. No, not every neutralized scip was once classed. Some were never in containment, some just don't tell us what they were classed before they were neutralized (I'm looking at you SCP-2682), and others, well, reasons.

So no, unclassed should not have been summarily removed from neutralized articles. I can't blame Roget for doing so, and I can't blame Aelanna for wanting to hit me upside the head for it. My apologies for creating the feeding ground for this debacle.

Lesson learned: do your fucking research before you ask someone to do something, Crayne.

I will now be going over neutralized articles to determine which ones need the unclassed tag.

Edit: ran through all neutralized articles, found none that had unclassed removed (save this one), but plenty that never got a class tag to begin with. Added S/E/K or unclassed where appropriate.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST
item.content:encoded: Already sent him a .tell :)

item.title: STAFF POST
item.content:encoded:
Aelanna, please stop reverting the page. Roget, ask Crayne to make a post, responding to this staff post, to clarify the situation at his earliest convenience.

God's sake people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the basis that:

  1. Regular users are allowed to make edits to correct errors,
  2. The Tag Guide has not yet been updated to reflect the changes that you claim are going to be made, and
  3. Any request or order from you automatically constitutes a conflict of interest,

I am refusing your request and reverting this page to its last correct state. If you want it to be wrong, then you can have another staff member make an order, or you can get the technical team leader to make your absurd change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've messaged Crayne to update it at his earliest convenience, and since it was a direct request from him to change the tags on the articles the way I did, so it should be right as rain soon :)

E: Please stop reverting the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that's true, then it's a hilariously self-centered and nonsensical decision. Either way, the Tag Guide isn't updated and thus you're still not in the right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I already did, I guess he didn't edit the tag guide yet. But I am in the right, as Crayne already told me to remove unclassed from the Neutralized pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: neutralized is a subclass and thus requires a main class. If no main class is implied by the contents of the page, then its main class is unclassed. If you're so determined to not have it be unclassed, then convince Crayne to change the tag definitions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mamoswine doesn't get Snow Warning, silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bit of a necro, but I wanted to state that the article does say it's a subspecies

So it's not saying all Mammoths, just this particular subspecies

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OK. Let me put this in perspective.

Under what logical rationale would it be believable to consider that a cloned Mammoth would cause temperatures around it to drop to at least -10°C when cloned from non-anomalous technology, and whose DNA was presumably not found to be anomalous as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree. I understand your position, but the foundation IS (as far as we know) mostly human. Do you really think, that, even the foundation, has nothing but perfectly run and maintained groups and branches?
Of course not
That's what makes it fun.
If you don't like it that's fine, but I just want to make sure you're aware that it IS possible for there to be a poorly ran branch, that might do something like this, and this idea is nothing new.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did the Foundation not set up any precautions while conducting this experiment? It's the FOUNDATION, not a silly amateur research facility. Down voted because of this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good article, short and sweet. I have two criticisms:

-The title of the image sounds… Lazy. I'd recommend making up something specific that that equipment does. If you want to keep it vague, use redactions. Right now, it's "this mysterious looking machinery is somehow related to the subject of this article." Why would the Foundation even attach that photo?

-The quote "development of the technology which resulted in the creation of SCP-2082 began…" directly conflicts with the earlier statement "SCP-2082 is an extinct subspecies of the animal…" since the Foundation did not actually create the SCP. Perhaps you should simply replace "creation" with "cloning."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the mammoths caused the ice age?

I don't really like it, because the places the mammoths were in were not dominated by ice and snow (granted, the source is Wikipedia, but it fits with what I've read in scientific literature about the mammoth).

Not to mention that if that's a risk, it would be easy enough to put the testing facility someplace far away from anything essential, pack a bunch of explosives right next to it, and trigger them to blow either remotely or in response to rapid temperature drop.

There's also already an SCP that causes mammoths. No ice involved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's in addition to the base area of effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall awesome, being big fan of mammoth, I just can't say otherwise to mammoth with overfreezing ability.

Except there is one error (I think) that really bug me.

there is an immediate alteration to the local temperature of every space within 250 meters of its body

Once SCP-2082's effect has covered an area greater than 20 square meters

If immediate alteration has radius of 250 meters from its body, then its immediate effected area is roughly 196312 square meters. Am I miss something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But I thought the Destroyers killed the dinosaurs… GOD DAMMIT PEOPLE STOP MAKING SKIPS THAT EXPLAIN EXTINCTION EVENTS! Regardless, I like it. +1

E: Okay, in retrospect, this is more about the ice age. Still though, I feel like any naturally occurring events are just explained by SCPs these days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How does the cold temperature stack?

Otherwise +1, nice idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cold snap, plus the lack of clothing, would be the reason for the death, probably. Form room temp to -10oC is around 30o difference in Celsius, around 55o difference in Fahrenheit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Surface temperatures will instantly drop to -10 °C, usually resulting in the death of any subjects within range.

That seems… Not that cold. That's 14 degrees fahrenheit. It's cold, but you could easily survive in that sort of environment with some cold weather clothes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like this if it did… anything interesting, basically. You have a great setup that you squander on having the mammoth just make shit cold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some people think this SCP is irrelevant. But it's not irrelevant, it's a mammoth!

…

I'll show myself out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually wrote this before the 2000 contest ended, before I wrote my 2000 entry, because I wanted this slot, because it's a They Might Be Giant's song. Voct helped me a lot because he always does and he is a handsome gentleman.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this because it's just a "we made it, it fucked us up, and then it burned out" event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the cold snap that kills, coupled with a lack of proper protection from the elements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Surface temperatures will instantly drop to -10 °C, usually resulting in the death of any subjects within range.

Is there a typo in this sentence? It was -30 °C back in december here and it didn't kill me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. One thing:

by Incident 2000-Prime.

Should this be "by Incident 2082-Prime"?

+1, regardless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the implication of "we dun fucked up".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An ice age causing wooly mammoth? Was this actually a result of the loning method, or was it a natural property of the wooly mammoth? Either way iI like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wasn't bad, I suppose. I didn't love it, but it's good.

Just so we're clear, it's a wooly mamoth that freezes this around it, correct?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will localize the image once I get home, or if anyone could be a dear and get it for me I would appreciate it.
